rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF HANS HERBERT BARTSCH
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Respondent Arndt Peltners Notice Of Motion For Summary Judgment Or, Alternatively, Summary Adjudication Of Issues Re: Petition To Determine Distribution Rights [Ccp 437c]
rulings.Rulings: Preliminarily, the Court notes that while Peltner could bring a separate motion or petition asking the Court to exercise its discretion under § 11604 to invalidate the assignment to Herterich, it would be improper to do so in the context of summary judgment. Thus, the request to invalidate the assignment is denied without prejudice to Peltner bringing a stand alone motion with the requisite showing per Probate Code 11604(c) to justify invalidating the assignment. With respect to laches, the Court notes that neither party addressed the holding in Drake v. Pinkham (2013) 217 Cal.App.4th 400 and its applicability to this matter. The Court further notes that while Buhrmann received notice of the various proceedings, there is no evidence that he had actual or constructive knowledge of e.g., Peltner's relationship with the Decedent or the role Peltner played in the preparation of the Decedent's estate plan, or other facts that would alert him to the existence of a possible claim against Peltner. The statute of limitations proffered by Peltner (Probate Code § 8270) clearly does not apply as former Probate Code § 21356 sets forth a clear timeline within which actions under former section 21350 must be brought: "at any time after letters are first issued to a general representative and before an order for final distribution is made." (See Murray Co. v. California Occupational Safety and Health Appeals Bd. (2009) 180 Cal. App. 4th 43, 53 [when two statutes relate to the same subject, the more specific and particular provision "will govern as against the more general provision, although the latter, standing alone, is broad enough to include the subject addressed by the more particular provision."].) The Petition has been brought within the specified time frame, and thus the statute of limitations does not apply to bar Herterich's claims. Peltner makes no effort to distinguish between res judicata and collateral estoppel, and/or to apply the specific requirements of those doctrines to the facts of this case. Nonetheless, the Court finds that neither doctrine applies, and that both summary judgment and summary adjudication on the basis of "collateral estoppel/res judicata" are inappropriate. Hearing required.
